where-am-i.label: Where Am I? pwd and ls
where-am-i.summary: Use `pwd`, `ls`, and hidden-file listing to understand the current workspace.

sketch0.title: pwd tells you your current location

sketch2.title: Relative paths start from where you are

try_where_am_i_sketch0.title: Print your current path
try_where_am_i_sketch0.prompt: Run `pwd` and confirm the path ends inside the workspace.

try_where_am_i_sketch1.title: Look inside a folder
try_where_am_i_sketch1.prompt: Run `ls desk` and look for `notes.txt` and `schedule.txt`.

sc-pwd-meaning.title: Which command shows your current folder?
sc-pwd-meaning.prompt: The command name means print working directory.
sc-pwd-meaning.hint: The command name means print working directory.

help.hint_2: Review the command names from the lesson, then choose the option that matches the task.

ci-create-navigation-lab.title: Check the workspace location
ci-create-navigation-lab.prompt: Run `pwd` and confirm the terminal shows the workspace path.
ci-create-navigation-lab.hint: Type `pwd`, press Enter, then check for `/workspace` in the response.

help.concept: `pwd` prints the folder where the terminal is currently working.

ci-create-visible-hidden-practice.title: List a nearby folder
ci-create-visible-hidden-practice.prompt: Run `ls campus` and look for `map.txt` and `room.txt`.
ci-create-visible-hidden-practice.hint: Type `ls campus` so you can inspect that folder without moving into it.

help.concept: `ls folder-name` lists the contents of that folder from where you are.
help.hint_2: Submit after the output shows `map.txt` and `room.txt`.

ci-create-long-list-lab.title: Reveal the hidden room note
ci-create-long-list-lab.hint: The `-a` option shows names that start with a dot.

help.concept: Files that start with `.` are hidden from a plain `ls`, but `ls -a` shows them.
help.hint_2: Submit after `.room-number` appears in the output.

try-location-print-path.title: Print your current path
try-location-print-path.prompt: Run `pwd` and confirm the path ends inside the workspace.
try-location-print-path.hint: Type `pwd` and read the full path.

help.concept: `pwd` helps you confirm your current location before choosing the next command.
help.hint_1: Type `pwd` and read the full path.
help.hint_2: Run the command in the terminal, check the response, then submit.

try-location-list-desk.title: Look inside a folder
try-location-list-desk.prompt: Run `ls desk` and look for `notes.txt` and `schedule.txt`.

help.concept: `ls folder-name` lists a folder without moving into it.
help.hint_2: Run the command in the terminal, check the response, then submit.

try-location-show-hidden-desk.title: Show hidden desk notes
try-location-show-hidden-desk.hint: Use `ls -a desk` so hidden names appear.

help.concept: The `-a` option includes hidden names that start with a dot.
help.hint_1: Use `ls -a desk` so hidden names appear.
help.hint_2: Run the command in the terminal, check the response, then submit.

pwd-current-place.title: `pwd` answers “where am I?”
pwd-current-place.bodyMarkdown:
`pwd` prints the folder your terminal is using right now. That matters because commands like `touch report.txt` create files in the current folder unless you give a longer path.

Try this rhythm often:

```bash
pwd
ls
```

First find where you are, then decide what to change.

ls-shows-nearby-files.title: `ls` shows what is nearby
ls-shows-nearby-files.bodyMarkdown:
`ls` lists the visible files and folders in the current location. You can also list a folder by name:

```bash
ls campus
```

That lets you inspect a place before moving into it or changing it.

paths-start-from-here.title: Hidden files still count
paths-start-from-here.bodyMarkdown:
Linux hides names that start with a dot from a plain `ls`. Use `ls -a` when you need the full picture.

```bash
ls -a
```

Hidden files often store settings, notes, or tool state.
